name: Northern Virginia Data Center Cluster (Data Center Alley)
operator: Amazon Web Services, Microsoft, Google, Meta
water_source: Potomac River watershed / Loudoun Water utility
notes: Largest data center market in the world. Loudoun County hosts over 25% of global internet traffic. Ongoing expansion is straining the county power grid.

name: Microsoft Iowa Data Center Campus
water_source: Raccoon River / Iowa American Water
notes: Microsoft drew controversy in 2023 after Iowa data centers consumed 6.4 million gallons of water in a single month during a drought.

name: Google Council Bluffs Data Center
water_source: Missouri River / Metropolitan Utilities District
notes: One of Google's oldest US data centers. Expanded multiple times since 2007.

name: Amazon Web Services (AWS) Oregon Region
water_source: Columbia River
notes: Boardman became a hub for AWS due to cheap hydroelectric power and Columbia River water access. The region has faced criticism for water draw during drought years.

name: Meta Prineville Data Center
water_source: Ochoco Irrigation District / groundwater
notes: Meta's first owned data center. Local officials raised concerns about water use in an already drought-stressed region.

name: Google Mayes County Data Center
water_source: Pryor Lake / Grand River Dam Authority
notes: Oklahoma incentivized Google with major tax breaks. The facility draws from Grand Lake o' the Cherokees watershed.

name: Microsoft Phoenix Data Center Campus
water_source: Colorado River / Salt River Project
notes: Located in one of the most water-stressed regions in the US. Arizona is facing severe Colorado River allocation cuts.

name: Google Mesa Data Center
water_source: Colorado River / Salt River Project
notes: Built in a desert region heavily dependent on the Colorado River, which is under historic drought stress.

name: Amazon Web Services Northern Virginia Expansion
water_source: Occoquan Reservoir / Prince William County Service Authority
notes: Proposed 2,000+ acre campus near Manassas Battlefield sparked major community opposition. Virginia approved it in 2023 despite protests over sprawl, traffic, and water.

name: Microsoft Wisconsin Data Center Campus
water_source: Lake Michigan / Racine Water and Wastewater Utility
notes: $3.3 billion investment. Located on former farmland. Community groups raised concerns about water use from Lake Michigan and displacement of agricultural land.

name: Meta DeKalb Data Center
water_source: Kishwaukee River / City of DeKalb water system
notes: Meta received significant tax incentives from Illinois to build here.

name: Google Columbus Data Center
water_source: City of New Albany water system
notes: Part of Ohio's growing data center corridor, subsidized by state tax breaks.

name: Amazon Web Services Ohio Region
water_source: Scioto River / Columbus Division of Power
notes: AWS's primary eastern US region. Significant tax incentives from Ohio.

name: Google Berkeley County Data Center
water_source: Lake Moultrie / Berkeley County Water and Sanitation
notes: One of Google's early US data centers, chosen for cheap hydro power from Santee Cooper.

name: Meta Fort Worth Data Center
water_source: Trinity River / Fort Worth Water Department
notes: Texas's deregulated power grid and lack of corporate income tax have made it a top data center destination, but extreme heat stress and grid instability are growing concerns.

name: Microsoft San Antonio Data Centers
water_source: Edwards Aquifer / San Antonio Water System
notes: Draws from the Edwards Aquifer, one of the most over-stressed aquifers in the US.

name: Google Midlothian Data Center
water_source: Ennis Municipal Water Supply
notes: Part of Texas's booming data center corridor south of Dallas-Fort Worth.

name: Amazon Web Services Proposed Nuclear-Powered Campus
water_source: Susquehanna River (Susquehanna Steam Electric Station)
notes: Amazon signed a deal to buy power directly from a nuclear plant, reviving debate about co-locating data centers with nuclear power stations. Regulators raised safety concerns.

name: Google Reno Data Center
water_source: Truckee River / Truckee Meadows Water Authority
notes: Nevada's low corporate taxes and cheap power have attracted major tech firms, but Reno is in a water-stressed basin.

name: Switch Las Vegas SuperNAP
water_source: Colorado River / Southern Nevada Water Authority
notes: One of the largest data center campuses in the world, located in the most water-stressed large metro in the US. The Colorado River, which supplies Las Vegas, is at crisis levels.

name: Microsoft Phoenix Proposed Campus Expansion
water_source: Colorado River / New River water system
notes: Proposed expansion amid Arizona's ongoing water emergency. Local groups are challenging the project's water permits.

name: Google Papillion Data Center
water_source: Missouri River / Metropolitan Utilities District
notes: Nebraska offered significant property tax incentives.

name: Amazon Proposed Indiana Data Centers
water_source: White Lick Creek watershed / Indiana American Water
notes: Part of AWS's ongoing Midwest expansion. Community groups in Boone County raised concerns about agricultural land conversion.

name: Meta Eagle Mountain Proposed Data Center
water_source: Utah Lake / Jordan Valley Water Conservancy District
notes: Proposed for a rapidly growing suburb. Utah Lake is under significant ecological stress, and environmental groups challenged the water rights application.

name: Microsoft Quincy Data Centers
water_source: Columbia River / Grant County PUD
notes: Quincy was transformed from a farming community into a data center hub. Cheap Columbia River hydropower was the draw, but farmers have raised concerns about competition for water.
